title: Carved ash ladle
subtitle: A long-handled soup ladle carved from a green ash billet with axe and hook knife
excerpt: A ladle is the most demanding of the kitchen carving projects: the bowl must be deep enough to hold a full portion of liquid, the handle long enough to reach safely into a pot, and the junction between them must carry the weight without flexing. Green ash is the traditional species: strong along the grain, manageable with axe and knife, and durable once oiled. A project for carvers who have already made at least one spoon.
sourceNotes: Ladle and long-handled kitchen utensil forms documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885); ash kitchen implements in British rural domestic craft tradition.

content.text: A ladle asks more of the carver than any other kitchen tool. The handle is long, which makes the piece awkward to hold and brace. The bowl is deep, which calls for steady
work. The join between handle and bowl must carry the weight of a full scoop of hot liquid without flexing or cracking.
ash is the right starting material. It rives cleanly. The long straight grain gives the handle its strength. It cuts readily under axe and knife before it dries.

from a section of trunk or large branch at least 12 cm in diameter. The billet should be thick enough in cross-section that the full bowl diameter (10 to 11 cm) and depth (5 to 6 cm) can be carved without breaking through the back. Mark the bowl end of the billet with the intended bowl diameter before riving; the rive should pass through the trunk leaving sufficient wood on the bowl side.

A carving axe; a sharp sloyd knife; a hook knife with a deep curve. A leather strop for all three. A 1000-grit waterstone for the hook knife (not optional on a ladle; the hook knife will need honing during the session, not just stropping, because the bowl work is extended). Sandpaper to 240 grit for the handle, optional on the outside if the knife finish is clean. Raw linseed oil.

Mark the ladle profile on the riven face: a long straight handle (30 cm) tapering from the handle end, then a relatively short transition zone (3 to 4 cm), then a wide oval bowl (10 to 11 cm diameter). Axe the sides of the handle down to just outside the pencil line. At the bowl end, the axe removes waste from both sides of the oval but leaves substantial material above the bowl floor line.

to bring the handle to an oval section, about 20 by 15 mm. A ladle handle needs to be comfortable to grip through oven gloves, which means slightly larger in section than a spoon handle. The handle bends slightly upward from the bowl end: this natural angle brings the bowl level when the handle is gripped and the forearm is horizontal. Produce this angle by angling the axe cuts at the bowl junction rather than trying to bend the handle after the fact.

The bowl exterior (the back face) should be smoothly domed: a shallow curve from rim to base. Use push cuts from the rim toward the base, working all the way round, to produce an even dome. The bowl walls at the rim should be 6 to 7 mm thick before hollowing starts. Thinner walls risk fracture during the hook-knife work.

Brace the ladle handle against the thigh and grip the bowl in the off-hand. With the hook knife, work from the centre of the bowl outward in short scooping cuts. The target depth is 5 to 6 cm at the centre. Work slowly: removing too much at once thins the walls unevenly. Rotate the bowl frequently to keep the cutting angle consistent with the grain.
At the bowl sides, grain direction reverses; check by feeling whether cuts run smooth or rough and reverse direction at the grain change. At the junction where bowl meets handle, use a

to define the boundary before removing material beyond it. The hook knife will need honing on the waterstone once or twice during this step; do not try to continue with a dull edge on the inside bevel.
Step 5: Check depth and wall consistency

Place a straight edge across the bowl rim and use a thin skewer or wire to measure depth at several points across the floor. The floor should be level or very nearly so. Press all around the outside of the bowl with fingertip pressure: any thin spot will flex. Mark thin spots and take corrective passes on the thick side rather than the thin.

Dry the ladle in a paper bag for seven days. After drying, take a final light knife pass on the outside if needed, or sand the handle to 240 grit. Apply raw linseed oil inside and out, allow to soak for one hour, and wipe off all surface oil. Cure for two weeks before food contact.

Raw linseed oil applied generously inside the bowl and worked into the wood with a fingertip. Wipe all surfaces dry after one hour. The ladle is food-safe two weeks after oiling. Re-oil at least twice a year; a ladle used for hot soups and stews loses oil faster than a spoon.
Make a smaller ladle with a 7 cm bowl for serving sauces. The process is the same at smaller scale. It is much faster. Good as a second go if the full ladle proves too hard on the first try.
Carve the handle tip into a hanging loop by drilling a hole or carving through the end once the rest of the ladle is finished.

Hand wash in warm water and dry immediately. Never dishwasher or soak. A ladle used for hot cooking absorbs more heat than a serving spoon; inspect the junction between handle and bowl every few months for any developing cracks. Re-oil promptly if the wood looks pale or dry.

items.fix: Sight along the ladle from the handle end in good light. Identify the high side at the junction and take corrective push cuts until the handle runs straight.
items.cause: The axe cuts at the junction did not produce a smooth transition; one face of the junction is thicker than the other, bending the handle.
items.symptom: The handle bends at the junction with the bowl and the ladle sits at an awkward angle

items.fix: A rim crack can sometimes be stabilised with thin cyanoacrylate glue wicked into the crack; wet the crack with the glue and hold closed for sixty seconds. For future work, leave rim walls at 6 to 7 mm before drying and use a paper bag.
items.cause: Drying was too rapid, or the wall thickness at the rim is too thin.
items.symptom: The bowl cracks at the rim during drying

items.fix: Hone the inside bevel on the 1000-grit stone every twenty to thirty minutes of sustained bowl work. On green ash, stropping alone is usually not enough for a full ladle hollow.
items.cause: The inside bevel was not honed on the waterstone before starting, or the green ash is denser than expected.
items.symptom: The hook knife cannot maintain sharpness through the bowl hollow
